title: Analyze a Phishing Email
description: Examine email headers and identify red flags.
problemStatement:
# Objective
Analyze the provided raw email headers and content to identify phishing indicators.

## Provided Email (Simulated)
```
From: "PayPal Service" <service@paypal.com>
Reply-To: paypal-update@secure-login.tk
Subject: URGENT: Your Account Has Been Limited
Date: Mon, 20 Oct 2024 10:23:45 -0500
To: victim@example.com

Dear Customer,

We noticed unusual activity on your account. Please verify your information immediately to avoid suspension.

Click here to restore access: http://paypal.com.verify-id.tk/login.php

Thanks,
PayPal Security Team
```

## Tasks
1.  Identify the **display name spoofing** tactic used.
2.  Analyze the **Reply-To** address. What domain is it actually using? Is it legitimate?
3.  Examine the **link** in the email body. What is the true domain? What technique is being used to hide it?
4.  List **three** additional red flags present in the email body text.

## Deliverable
A short list of the 4 findings above.
hints: Look at the part after the '@' symbol in the Reply-To field.
The link text shows 'paypal.com' but the actual href goes to a different domain.
expectedOutput: 1. Display name 'PayPal Service' but email not verified. 2. Reply-To uses .tk domain (free, suspicious). 3. Link goes to verify-id.tk, a typosquat/lookalike domain. 4. Red flags: Urgency, generic greeting, grammar issues, threat of suspension.
solutionCode:
# Analysis
1. Display name 'PayPal Service' but actual From address is missing/not shown; likely spoofed or from a free account.
2. Reply-To domain is 'secure-login.tk'. '.tk' is a free domain often used in scams. Not PayPal.com.
3. Link text is 'http://paypal.com.verify-id.tk/login.php'. The actual domain is 'verify-id.tk' (paypal.com is a subdomain). This is a subdomain attack.
4. Red flags: Urgency ('URGENT'), generic greeting ('Dear Customer'), threat ('avoid suspension'), grammatical oddness.
